title: Gender Representation in Economics across Topics and Time: Evidence from the NBER

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of New York staff report examines gender representation among authors at NBER Summer Institute conferences from 2001 to 2016. The study finds women represented 20.6% of authors over 2013-16, with representation varying significantly by subfield: 14.4% in finance, 16.3% in macroeconomics, and 25.9% in microeconomics.
key_points: Women comprised 20.6% of authors at NBER Summer Institute papers during 2013-16 period
Subfield variation: 14.4% female authors in finance, 16.3% in macroeconomics, 25.9% in microeconomics
Acceptance rates for papers by women are statistically indistinguishable from those by men
Female author share is comparable to share of women among tenure-track professors but 10 percentage points below assistant professor representation
Programs organized by women show higher shares of female authors and discussants
Published October 2017 as Staff Report No. 825
why_it_matters: This research documents diversity patterns in academic economics conferences. It does not address market structure, trading dynamics, or macro catalysts and falls outside the archive's defined scope.
important_numbers: 10 percentage point gap vs. assistant professor representation

issues_found: Source topic (gender representation in academic economics conferences) is not relevant to market structure or macro catalysts as defined in task scope
Source is a research paper on academic diversity at NBER conferences - no content about trading mechanisms, market plumbing, liquidity, microstructure, or macro economic catalysts
Published October 2017 - over 8 years before TODAY (2026-03-31) and lacks clear relevance to justify archival retention for specified purposes

human_review.notes: Source is factually accurate but fundamentally outside the defined scope of market structure and macro catalyst research. While the record correctly captures the source content, the source itself does not address any topics related to trading dynamics, market microstructure, liquidity, or macro catalysts (Fed decisions, rate changes, economic data releases). The TARGET and TOPIC metadata claim 'market structure' but the actual content concerns gender representation in academic economics conferences.

notes: IRRELEVANT TO SPECIFIED TOPICS. This staff report concerns gender representation among academic economists at NBER conferences, not market structure or macro catalysts. Source content is factually captured but falls outside task scope. Historical publication (2017) - not recent. MARKET IMPACT: none - not applicable to financial markets.
